item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Fair Use claim denied. Image has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
" he claims that he spent several thousand dollars on concessions during the screening and apologizes to the audience for the 12-week hiatus that At the Movies took while he and Siskel were attending the screening. "

I love it.

item.title: Image - Fair Use Claim
item.content:encoded:
Smapti wrote the text for this, but I grabbed the original image.

Obviously this image is not CC. However, I would like to claim fair use on it given that:

  • The image is a smaller screenshot of the original broadcast
  • The image is a lower quality replication of the original broadcast
  • It is used in the contest of a transformative dervitive work

If necessary, the size of the image can be reduced to comply with fair use criteria.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I desperately want to know what the Siskel/Ebert review of The Room would be like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What about Undertale? Will it be like Mass Effect and they each see a different route?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Overall, an interesting SCP. An idea: insert a disc containing Minecraft. It has no story. >:D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, this was such a great read, +1.

On a personal note, it was both wonderful and bittersweet to find out we have a Roger Ebert SCP! I'm too young to have known Siskel, but I spent my late teens and early twenties avidly reading Ebert's reviews, especially his Great Movies series. He taught me more about films than anyone else. I miss him, and still can't get over the fact that he'll never publish another review…and now I'm sad. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bravo! +1

I laughed at the Mass Effect one, then after the final test with 1989, I wondered what would happen if they tested it on 1981 (Ronald Reagan Cut Up While Talking). But then I remembered 1981 is a VHS tape, not a disc. Still, interesting idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel bad because I laughed at 911.
Please don't take that out of context.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh, I remember back when I first posted this ages ago. Still grateful to Smapti for rescuing a concept I didn't execute very well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: man eaten by the idea of a shark sounds like a blockbuster hit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Extremely creative, +1 all the way :)
I'd like to think that somewhere, Gene and Roger are giving two thumbs up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda sorta had AWCY in mind when I rewrote this, but I left it deliberately vague. Ebert could be referring to anyone here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I see "artistic collective" 'round these parts, I think AWCY?.

But on full reading I figured that AWCY? wasn't intended here, and that the "artistic collective" in this case was the Foundation. Was I mistaken?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beat me to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sure he's with Gene in Heaven right now and they're arguing about GI Joe: Retaliation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still really sad about this. He was my single biggest writing influence, especially when it came to my movie reviews.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for all the laughs, rages, and insights that you provided each and every one of us,
It's been a wonderful ride Roger.
RIP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, yes we do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rest in peace, Roger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Roger Ebert has died.

This article is now dedicated to his memory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
>_>

<_<

You didn't see nuthin'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was a dumb on my part.

I changed my downvote to an upvote after a reread, but I have to ask: Why didn't either Siskel or Ebert talk about the quality of the Mass Effect graphics? If they're treating it as a CGI film, it seems like they would at least talk about the CGI. If this is modern times, they'd probably criticise it (Because it has worse CGI than today's fully animated films) while they'd probably congratulate it if Mass Effect was released back when the show was on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I give this article a thumbs up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That leaves "e b e r"

Beer.

YOU'VE BEEN OUSTED, BEER ROGET!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For me, it was 1756-28, with the footage from 9/11 and the comment about people playing themselves convincingly which made me upvote.

Also, I noticed something.
Roger Ebert
Roget.
SUPERMAN, I KNOW YOUR SECRET! *evil laughter.*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was good, and then I hit Experiment 1756-17 and it suddenly got great. Good cross-test, as well (also Man Being Eaten By the Idea of a Shark; I want this).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I am used to the usual internet where most people confuse opinion and fact and say something sucks as a fact and later say it's only an opinion, me being so used to that I couldn't help but make the connection here

and now I'm going to shut up before this goes out of hand

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCPs don't need plot twists, they're supposed to be anomalous objects with interesting characteristics

That only works when your idea and anomalous characteristics are interesting enough to support an article by themselves. This isn't.

In my opinion, a good SCP is a narrative. It should build up to something. See 1983 and 089 for perfect examples of this. This isn't that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
what Rumetzen is saying is that ALL SCPs that simply show the anomalous properties and what happens are bad

Um, he's not. He's just stating that he himself is very much opposed to those sorts of SCPs. Hence

Meh. It's supported entirely by the experiment logs, which is something that always bothers me, and it's another example of the "telling you what this thing does and then demonstrating it with no twist" type of SCP I loathe.

He's making it rather straightforward that these are his own personal opinions, as opposed to just offering blanket statements.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah I understand that but when looking at SCPs like 902, those have HOOKS they don't require some special plot twists that totally alter your perception of the SCP in question, I know what you're saying, but sometimes just seeing what the SCP does in various situations is interesting enough, what Rumetzen is saying is that ALL SCPs that simply show the anomalous properties and what happens are bad; which is something I argue against

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCPs don't need plot twists, they're supposed to be anomalous objects with interesting characteristics,

Some people prefer having such a twist (not sure how related the "twist" would be to a "hook", but both are nice) in SCP articles because then the reader itself thinks beyond what is immediately in front of them, and the realization makes the article memorable.

Also, I'd say maybe lay off the authoritative comments until you've written a successful SCP yourself? Technically, there's already an entire page of anomalous items with interesting characteristics, that aren't SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCPs don't need plot twists, they're supposed to be anomalous objects with interesting characteristics, I personally think that this did its job and explained pretty well what it does while still keeping it interesting, there really is no need for a plot twist

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh. It's supported entirely by the experiment logs, which is something that always bothers me, and it's another example of the "telling you what this thing does and then demonstrating it with no twist" type of SCP I loathe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea of the Mass Effect section was to show what would happen if they were given a "movie" where major plot elements are subject to change. Siskel "watched" a playthrough where Shepard was a male Paragon, and Ebert saw a version where Shepard was a female Renegade and romanced Liara (but Kaiden dies in both their versions).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Who says it's an AWCY reference? Two of the objects mentioned in that review are MC&D items.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Mass Effect part made me cringe. It feels too much like a Mass Effect fan's opinion instead of an outsider's opinion, and the entire point of this article is that it's an outsider's opinion; reviewers tend to be that. I like the way there is a split between their opinions based on the two potential ways one can make their character develop, but Ebert comparing fem-shep to Sigourney Weaver is pretty indulgent.

I like the basic idea, it's just that one entry that really rubbed me the wrong way. Also, the AWCY reference is pretty forced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like the idea very much. It's a DVD player that appears to contain digital versions of Ebert and Siskel. The AWCY reference adds absolutely nothing, other than linking what is clearly an MC&D SCP to AWCY. The testing logs don't really interest me at all. Even though it's a bad idea that isn't done too badly, it's still a concept that I don't like.

Although I like the idea of the pair of them reviewing two different Shepards.

Still -1. I don't think the concept is redeemable, personally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The point of it is to show that they'll treat even recordings of anomalous objects like they're just regular films

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Mass Effect review made me smile.

I liked the idea when it was first posted, and this rewrite makes it so much better

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely loved this until that last test. You still have my upvote, but the AWCY critique adds nothing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, we do. Perhaps this document was not written for personnel with high enough clearance for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do we even need to Redact "Are We Cool Yet?" I think we all know who he's talking about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the third time I've upvoted this style of article. The next one is gonna have to knock it outta the park to get anything but a downvote from me.

Overall, it's good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Smapti y u always ride on coattails. :<

I mean, it's a well written and entertaining article, but I'm getting a feeling of deja vu.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely improved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One minor criticism: Siskel & Ebert didn't give "neutral votes"; it was always thumbs up or down. Recommend changing that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's the details that make this one. Funny, but still takes the subject matter seriously.

Nice to see AWCY? redacted for once, not a hundred percent certain on connecting it with SCP-1989. Not a big issue.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I said in chat: Interesting, an improvement over the original, but I feel it still has unexplored potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And what a rewrite! My original concept was good, and I wanted to do more with it but didn't know how. The different experiments you explore in this are much better than what I could have come up with. Your description and containment procedures were a lot more professionally written than mine were, and the experiment logs were definitely in character for the two critics. :P

Two Thumbs Up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rewrite of the previous SCP-1756 by ObserverSeptember.
